key_insights.insight: Confidence, as culturally defined, is largely based on evidence, making action essential to build it.
key_insights.quote: Confidence as we describe it in culture is really just, is based on the evidence you have in yourself.

key_insights.insight: Anxiety is predicting failure and confidence is predicting success, our expectations heavily influence outcomes.
key_insights.quote: If you imagine confidence on a spectrum with anxiety on one end and confidence on the other anxiety is predicting failure and confidence is predicting success

key_insights.insight: Audacity, putting your head above the parapet, is an essential element for progress and success.
key_insights.quote: You have to be audacious you have to put your head above the parapet to really you know put yourself forward

key_insights.insight: It's vital to understand and draw upon deep-seated pain points because these fuel motivation more effectively than superficial desires.
key_insights.quote: The pain points are deep and people need to draw on those because the day that you're getting out of bed and you feel like shit and you're tired and you want to give up, I want to be toned isn't going to do it.

key_insights.insight: The key to confidence is being happy to lose; failure is an inherent element.
key_insights.quote: The key to confidence is being happy to lose… Confidence is much more of a relationship to failure.

key_insights.insight: Losing isn't the same as being defeated; the ability to turn up and try again matters.
key_insights.quote: Losing is not the same as being defeated…you can lose, but if you turn up and you go again, you've not been defeated.

key_insights.insight: The reason why people aren't doing stuff that they think they want to do, isn't because of a lack of confidence, it is a lack of belief.
key_insights.quote: I think they want to believe they can do it, but they don't truly believe it.

key_insights.insight: Consider the 'utility of deprivation,' sacrificing immediate gratifications can benefit other areas of your life.
key_insights.quote: To stop drinking, for instance, will have a net positive on other areas of your life... there is a utility and a benefit to depriving yourself of them.

actionable_advice.advice: Practice discomfort: Ask for a 10% discount on your coffee to feel uncomfortable and realize how much we think people are paying attention.
actionable_advice.how_to: Next time you order a cup of coffee, ask for a 10% discount, understanding that you're unlikely to receive it, but the act itself is the objective.

actionable_advice.advice: Identify and work on inadequacies, as they reveal the path to progression and passion.
actionable_advice.how_to: Pinpoint a specific inadequacy and commit to a small, manageable step to address it, like asking for a small pay rise.

actionable_advice.advice: Turn the dial toward optimism by changing your internal narrative.
actionable_advice.how_to: If you're about to go on a date, shift your thought from 'What if this is the worst date?' to 'What if this is the best date?'

actionable_advice.advice: Break down bigger goals into smaller, manageable steps.
actionable_advice.how_to: Instead of aiming for the macro goal of eating healthier or moving more, break that down into micro habits such as no food until 1pm.

memorable_stories: Liver King's crippling social anxiety despite his public persona.
James's worst day in Australia involving messaging his dad for money and buying a whiteboard that changed everything.
Stephen's epiphany about asking questions in a minivan on Croatia.
contrarian_claims: Manifestation is a very dangerous thing, focusing on thinking about success alone is not enough.
Confidence isn't a personality trait but more of a set of values that each person can have.
Striving for incompletatble goals to avoid ant-climax.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - James Smith: Become Confident In 100 Minutes
